Well, unless I haven't had a chance to meet you yet, I'm Lori Haller and I own my own graphic design studio in Washington, D.C. In my studio, it's full service and what that means is I don't just do this direct mail, I do package design, logos, mass teds, a lot of websites now, emails and you know, anything that really comes up. So what happens, just so you understand if you're perhaps just getting into copywriting or want to get into this line of work, is that sometimes clients come to me and this is what Clayton was just saying, they will come to me now and say, you know, Lori, we have this new launch, it's this new supplement with like pomegranate in it and we really want to have you design it and who have you been working with lately, wink, wink, that you know, maybe has some controls with you and you work together as a unified team. So that happens a lot to me now and then if I already had worked with you or knew you in the past, I would go ahead and tell them like they asked for three or four people. So that can happen. The opposite happens where it's like if you are the copywriter, then perhaps you've heard of me or saw some of my winners in the past and that way it works vice versa. So what Clayton had to say is very important that we work hand in hand together. So what can happen is we'll all get together and talk about like what the product is, what it's going to do for the person and all the specifications. The copywriter will go away obviously and write the copy but I on the other hand, and this is something that you might not know yet about graphic designers, you go in and do all of your research, you know, the unique benefits, the specificities, what is it going to cost, who's going to look at it, I do the same thing. Like in a huge annoying way because most of my clients are exhausted when I get done with them in my big list, they tell me this all the time and you can hear them sigh on the phone after they see my email. But I do that as well because it's very, very important for me, the designer who's going to paint this picture visually with your wonderful copy to really try to understand your copy, understand who it is that's going to read it and what do they need to see visually with your brilliant copy that you spent maybe three months on writing, what am I going to be able to tell them or show them visually that will like it's almost like a marriage. So I always try to tell people first off when I meet them and they're a copywriter that to me in my heart, deep in my heart and soul, copy is king. It's so important and important to me and everyone else in this room. But on the other side of the coin, design is the queen that matches up with that and they go through the kingdom and make it like magical together. I really, really, I think probably the design really does help. Obviously, I am very serious about that. So just quickly, Clayton, I was just going to show this particular piece that I brought with me and I'm very excited about a week before, like about a week ago, I just found out that my piece won the control, which means, and tell me if I'm saying this in the wrong way for copywriters, but in the past, there was a particular product for women my age. What does that mean? It's a face cream, a particular moisturizer, a specific one that's more like a gel. That stays on the top of the skin, but then it goes in. There's all these really peculiar things about this face cream. So they've been advertising it with a direct mail campaign, like such as this. And so these three campaigns here, and you can look at them like through the course of the seminar, but these three mailed, like I think this was like three years ago, and then this design and copy was a couple of years ago, and then this one was recently, and it won like the most. So it's the new control. So the copywriter, Kim Krause, Schwam and I go in, she writes a copy, we do this long series of meetings, I exhaust the client, drive them crazy with all my questions, and so we come up with this piece. So our piece just mailed, and we just got the information back. And like I said, this control is now going to be rolled out, which means maybe millions, this is for healthy directions. I don't know what their core list quantity is, but it'll be rolled out in June. And hopefully again and again and again, Kim and I will be called back to update this or create a new package together. So I'm hoping that helps solve the mystery, because those are the questions that came up last night and this morning at lunch. Where do I fit into the equation as graphic designer? So that's... I want you to talk a little bit about how you got into this and how you found your first client. Yes. All right. Well, I'll tell you really quickly how I first discovered that I was going to do this, or not be a race car driver, or some of those other things. I don't know, that was on the list, but when I was seven or eight and I was fooling around with, you know, you're starting to write letters and numbers and things like that, my grandma asked me to do these thank you notes. And she gave me all these little things and I knew how to write and letters and stuff. And so she's like, can you just make these thank you notes for me? And so probably everyone else in the room is like, what the heck is she, you know, what direction is she going with this? So probably for everyone else in here, you know, you just wrote out the letters, you drew a flower on it. I don't know. I started drawing these letters just to me. It's so personal. Like the A sender on the H and then the A is so round and then the, you know, all that stuff I'm like as a little kid, you know, really entranced by it. It's like, so it became my passion, my true passion. So, you know, I went on to art school and tried to get some jobs full time. Like some of the other people in the room, I had to learn, I mean, I had to learn from the best. So I went from agency to agency, you know, trying to find the best typographer, the best person with models, the best with film. I've done a lot of film, radio, TV and, you know, different directions. So I did that. But then I was like, I really want to have my own company. So I don't know. Maybe it was like 15 years ago, I started really just deciding I'm not going to work. And you guys might be in that position too. You know, you're working full time. You have this passion on the side and you're like, I want to jump over. And Carleen, you probably, I think we worked together when I was full time and I had told you that as well. Like I really want to just go out of my own. I want to have my own agency and try to accomplish my goals. So I did. I just told Walter Pierce, who was so lovely with me at KCI. And that's where I started working with Clayton's copy that I just, I want to give it a try on my own. And he was so behind me and so helpful. And perhaps this would be a good tip for you as well. You know, maybe you're working somewhere full time, you're doing copy or whatnot, and they will give you some clients as well. That's what I did. And 15 years later, you know, I'm still, you know, I've got three kids. They're still fed pretty much, you know, most of the time. No, but I've been very, very blessed and lucky to make that happen. But one of the things I wanted to tell you about is a story that's going to make me cry about Carleen. But no, so one idea or a little tip I want to give you, and this is sort of similar to what some of the other people have said, but it's a little different. There are, I'd say around eight of us women in this one area before Carleen moved away. It was myself, a designer, Beth Sokoloff from Phillips, also a mom, Carleen, and Kim, and two or three other women. And we decided that all of us had just jumped or had decided to jump soon. So like I still had a little one and some people were pregnant and we all decided to come once a month at least and sit and talk with each other at the Starbucks, you know, get a babysitter or whatever. So I had worked with Carleen at Georgetown publishing on stuff, and she had seen my work and we got to know each other, but she didn't know me, know me. You know, I have a unique way of working. My agency is very different. I go in kind of like as a businessman first, and then I'm the art girl on the side. You know, I want to increase the bottom line and have all these goals. She didn't know that about me. So during these, it was about a year and then you had to move away. Well, I know that was pretty bad. So Carleen moved away. So that was kind of my chance to allow her to get to know me. I like, you know, it was give and take with each person in the group. Beth, I ended up hiring her for a lot of projects. Kim and I started working on projects. The other woman, Lindsay Mains, you know, we would help each other. You guys, I'm stuck at home all day. I can't write my copy. Somebody would say, until late at night. The other woman, and this doesn't just have to be women, obviously. Twist the story around. It can be men. You don't have to meet in person. That's just what girls do kind of. But so what happened was Carleen ends up getting a call a couple years later from Oprah, and they had asked her to write the copy. And so Carleen needed an art director, and she called me. You know, there's a million art directors out there, and our joke is that Rob Davis, the king of art direction, wasn't, you know, available that day. So she was stuck. No one else was available, so she had to call Lori. But seriously, that wouldn't have happened to me if we wouldn't have done that. I've only known this much of me and my potential and what I stood for. I was able to, whether we did it on the phone or maybe a Skype now, you know, this is like 10 years or five years, you know, we didn't have Skype then, I was able to let her, you know, come inside and see what my company was like, see what my personality was like. And so I think that's the way that perhaps you guys could work together is getting to know each other and then say, you get a job, and then you need another copywriter. This just really worked well for me. And now, really, everybody will go on my website. That was just one project. It wasn't any different than this. I did the same thing. It just happened to be Oprah. So people are like, you know, I still do $25 business cards for the dry cleaner down the street, like you did Oprah, you know, like, oh yeah, that was me. So that probably, well, not just probably, that catapulted my career hugely. And I really thank Carline for that. In a huge way, I was able to hug. We got, oh yes. She's being very modest because when I got the assignment and they told me, they said, hey, we like you to, you know, let us know a couple or two or three designers that you like to work with. And I said, I only have one. I said, it's Lori Hallard. That's who I want to work with in there. So she's amazing because they want us to do something different and creative. And this, you're just seeing just a little bit of when Lori is. I mean, you're talking about, you know, just a large amount of enthusiasm and just passion for what she does. And I thought, wow, this is one of the biggest opportunities I have. I want somebody who has, who's going to be equally excited about this and crazy passionate about it. And so she says, you know, Rob Davis, Rob wasn't even in the running, although I love Rob. I love Rob Davis. I love him, but he wasn't. It was always Lori. And so when I called her. Okay, so I'm like. I don't know who it is on the phone, but it sounds good. Lori shared that story with me last night and I hadn't heard her perspective from that, you know, and it was very, you know, touching because we do have a friendship, a work relationship, and it's so nice when you're successful, that's wonderful, but it's also amazing to help others be successful. You get more joy really out of that. You look around and see people that, hey, we struggled together, and then when we're making it big together and we're helping one another, and so you can always find a way to help somebody no matter what your situation is. So it always pans out and always works out, so just find ways to do it. Definitely, no. Thank you, Carly. Thank you. And, you know, one last thing. The other woman there that I mentioned, Kim Kralse Schwam, who wrote this piece, so about three weeks ago, and I, for this whole year, my dream, you know how you wake up and you want a whole, you know, a dream, my dream for this whole year and moving in the next five years, I really have my heart set on getting clients in Paris, London, and New York. That's just, I don't know how that came to me, but I'm like, I am hell bent and I am going to get that, you know? So I like doing this thing where, well, you probably don't want to do this, but you could try it at home if you want to. It's like, put it out to the universe, you know? Tell everybody, like, you know, I tell people now, I'm trying to get clients in London, Paris, and New York. You know? Do you know anybody that needs art or whatever? So Kim calls me and she says, gosh, I'm doing this big, huge project, and I already knew it because when I kept going over to her house to work on this, we lived near each other. She kept giving me all these smoothies to try, and they were delicious, so she was working on this huge client in London, Greengate London, beautiful website, logo, package designed by a London agency. I was feeling really like, aw, you know? So I was looking at all of her stuff, and then she calls me and she says, I really hate to ask this of you, but the fancy London agency is too busy to put the copy into the layout. So, they were so, not snobby, but they were like, they don't want to put the copy in the layout and see how long the copy is or isn't or figure it out, they just wanted to do the fancy design. And at first, you know, my feelings were a little like, I wish I was a person, I'd love to have a London agency. You know, that's my dream. And I tried to pick myself up on my bootstraps, and I'm like, come on, just, okay, so I said, Kim, for free, we've been friends since these coffee things, we're kind of out loud. I will flow, it was like two or three documents, and also it was helping Kim. You know, I don't know where you are in your copy career, but she's like, I don't know if it's too long, too short. I don't know, it's an odd client, and it's supposed to be really sleek and these beautiful photographs and stuff like that. I'm like, please, let me do it. I figured out millimeters, yeah. And I went ahead and gave them to her for free, and no big deal, you know, it's two or three hours worth of work, I adore Kim. And so then she sent it to the client, they cut the copy, she was like so apologetic, she had to do it two or three times. Then it was going to the fancy London, you know, place. And lo and behold, the next day I get an email, and this woman, Inessa, that owns the company, she emailed me and she had heard what I had done, and she wanted to use me, you know? So, yeah, I was like, hot, dickety dog. So, I just finished that up, like, I'm not lying, three hours before I got on the plane. What I ended up doing, she had absolutely no budget, that's probably what happened. I sucked it up, I'm like, I don't care, you know, I want this client bad. So probably for one of the projects, I probably got, you know, that chunk of money probably paid for that. There were two other, so I pretty much worked my, you know, what off, for a good couple weeks. I also provided another idea, because these were brochures and whatnot, banners that were going to this huge trade show for these fancy health products. I'm like, Lori, there's 5,000 different attendees and vendors and expo's and people that are going there. You just knock yourself out on these, all those people are maybe going to ask Inessa, because I did a beautiful job, I know I did. Who did your art? So I gave her another free thing, I even wrote the copy on it, and I told her, how about if we do two times a day, these free raffle giveaways, and I just make these little raffles on a piece of 8 1⁄2 by 11 piece, you know, paper, you chop it, you give it away, you try to bring the people in there. They want all these free products and stuff, and so I put that together too, and she just emailed last night that everything is back from the printer. The show happens Sunday and Monday, and she has a lot of other things now she wants to talk to me about. So I will leave you with that. I wish you the best of luck, and I just find probably the best thing is giving back, giving that part of yourself back perhaps, and using your craft, and what you're really good at in life has really paid off for me. Good luck to you. Thanks for your time.
